1: hēn-kōl rāʾăṯāh ʿênî — 'lo, all of this my eye has seen' — the direct sensory claim; Job opens his reply asserting first-person ocular witness. šāmĕʿāh ʾāznî wattāḇen lāh — 'my ear has heard and understood it' — the use of both eye and ear establishes comprehensive apprehension; Job has perceived both the observable facts and their meaning. He is not speaking from ignorance but from full-sensory witness that matches or exceeds the friends'.
2: kĕdaʿtḵem yāḏaʿtî gam-ʾānî — 'what you know, I know too' — the emphatic gam-ʾānî ('even I') and the repetition of the root yāḏaʿ insist on equal epistemic standing. lō-nāp̄ēl ʾānōḵî mimmkem — 'I do not fall below you' — the same negated-nāpal construction as 12:3; the repetition of the claim across these two chapters suggests it is a rhetorical refrain challenging the friends' implicit intellectual superiority claim.
3: ʾûlām ʾănî ʾel-Šaddai ʾădabbēr — 'surely I want to speak to El Shaddai' — ûlām is an adversative particle marking a sharp contrast with what precedes: 'yes, I know what you know — but what I actually want is to argue before God directly.' wĕhôḵēaḥ ʾel-ʾĒl ʾeḥpōṣ — 'and to argue my case before El' — hôḵēaḥ is from yāḵaḥ (to rebuke, argue, adjudicate); Job is invoking the legal connotation explicitly. He wants not religious submission but forensic vindication.
4: tōp̄ĕlê šāqer — 'plasterers of falsehood' — tāp̄al is to plaster or coat over, used in Ezek 13:10-15 of prophets 'plastering' a flimsy wall with whitewash; the friends are applying a deceptive surface coating of doctrine to Job's situation without engaging its actual substance. rōp̄ĕʾê ʾĕlîl — 'physicians of worthlessness' — ʾĕlîl means 'nothingness/worthlessness' (the same word used for false gods in Lev 19:4; Isa 2:8); the friends' remedies are made of the same substance as idols — nothing.
5: mî-yittēn haḥărêš taḥărîšûn — 'O that you would indeed keep completely silent' — the infinitive absolute haḥărêš intensifies the verb taḥărîšûn: 'truly keep silent.' wĕtihî lāḵem lĕḥāḵmāh — 'and it would be wisdom for you' — ironic wisdom-maxim: in your case, the wiser path is to stop speaking. Prov 17:28 makes the same point explicitly ('even a fool is counted wise when he holds his tongue'); Job anticipates and inverts the proverbial form by applying it to ostensible sages.
6: šimĕʿû-nāʾ tôḵaḥtî — 'hear now my argument/reproof' — tôḵaḥat is the legal-wisdom term for rebuke, argument, or reproof (from yāḵaḥ, same root as v3); it appears in Prov 1:23; 29:15 for disciplinary correction. wĕriḇôṯ śĕp̄āṯay haqšîḇû — 'and give attention to the pleadings of my lips' — rîḇ is the formal legal dispute or lawsuit; Job is asking the friends to hear his actual legal argument rather than precluding it with theological frameworks.
7: hălĕʾêl tĕdabbĕrû ʿawlāh — 'will you speak injustice on God's behalf?' — ʿawlāh is ethical injustice/wrong, not merely falsehood; Job is charging the friends with advocacy that is morally corrupt, not just factually mistaken. ûlô tĕdabbĕrû rĕmiyyāh — 'and for him speak deceit' — rĕmiyyāh is slackness/deception (cf. Ps 52:2 'tongue like a sharp razor, working deceit'). The accusation is that the friends are willing to lie for God — a form of false witness.
8: hăp̄ānāyw tiśśĕʾûn — 'will you lift his face?' — nāśāʾ pānîm is the idiom for showing partiality/favoritism (lit. 'lifting the face' of the favored party); its prohibition runs through Lev 19:15; Deut 10:17; and the prophets. Job is accusing the friends of judicial bias — accepting God's side in the dispute without examining the evidence — which is precisely what law forbids. lāʾĒl tĕrîḇûn — 'will you contend for El?' — the same rîḇ vocabulary; they have appointed themselves God's lawyers in a case where God is a party.
9: hăṭôḇ kî-yĕḥqōr ʾetḵem — 'is it good that he should search you out?' — if God examines the friends as he might examine any disputant, their advocacy will be exposed as partial and corrupt. ʾim-kĕhāṯēl ʾĕnôš tĕhāṯĕlû-bô — 'or will you deceive him as one deceives a mortal?' — hāṯal means to mock/deceive by playing tricks; the question exposes the absurdity of attempting to deceive God the way one might trick a credulous human adversary.
10: hôḵēaḥ yôḵîaḥ ʾetḵem — 'he will certainly reprove you' — the doubled infinitive absolute (hôḵēaḥ yôḵîaḥ) emphasizes certainty of divine judgment against the friends; the same yāḵaḥ root that Job used in v3 (wanting to argue his own case before God) is now turned on the friends: the very divine court Job is invoking will find against them. ʾim-bĕsēṯer pānîm tiśśĕʾûn — 'if in secret you show favoritism' — the 'secret' aspect; what the friends do in their private reasoning, God exposes.
11: hălōʾ śĕʾēṯô tĕḇahēl ʾetḵem — 'will not his majesty terrify you?' — śĕʾēṯô is 'his rising/elevation' (from nāśāʾ), used as a term for divine majesty or the awesome upwelling of divine presence. wĕp̄aḥdô yippōl ʿălêḵem — 'and his dread fall upon you' — paḥad (dread/terror) falling as a weight on a person is an OT theophanic language pattern; the same idea governs the theophanic visions of Isaiah (Isa 2:10,19; 6:5) and Job's own encounter (23:15-16).
12: zikrōnêḵem mišlê ʾēp̄er — 'your wise maxims are proverbs of ash' — mišlê ʾēp̄er is a devastating compound: māšāl (proverb, wisdom saying) reduced to ʾēp̄er (ash/dust), the residue of burning. The friends' celebrated wisdom is literally the ash left after the fire of genuine insight has been extinguished. ligbê-ḥōmer gabbêḵem — 'your defenses/arguments are defenses of clay' — gaḇ (a raised defense or raised back?) modeled in clay (ḥōmer) — perishable, earthy, fragile.
13: haḥărîšû mimmennî wăʾăḏabbĕrāh ʾānî — 'be silent, leave me alone, and let me speak' — Job asks for silence from the friends so he can present his case directly to God. wĕyaʿăḇor ʿālay mah — 'and let whatever comes, come upon me' — the fatalistic openness is deliberate; Job is willing to face whatever divine response his direct confrontation brings. The clause functions as an act of existential courage: consequences accepted in advance.
14: maddûaʿ ʾeśśāʾ bĕśārî bĕšinnay wĕnap̄šî ʾāśîm bĕkappî — 'why do I take my flesh in my teeth and put my life in my hands?' — two idioms for extreme recklessness and mortal risk. Biting one's own flesh is a sign of desperation; 'putting one's life in one's hands' (nepeš + kap = palm) is a fixed idiom for risking death, used in Judg 12:3 and 1 Sam 19:5; 28:21. Job is asking rhetorically why he persists in a confrontation that could destroy him — yet proceeds anyway.
15: hēn yiqtĕlēnî lō ʾăyaḥēl — the most disputed verse in the book. The Masoretic text has a qere/ketib variant: the written text (ketib) has lō (negative particle) = 'though he kills me, I have no hope'; the marginal reading (qere) has lô (to him) = 'though he kills me, yet I will hope in him.' The KJV ('yet will I trust in him') follows the qere. In context the latter reading coheres better: v16 ('this will be my salvation') and the whole trajectory of chs. 13–14 point toward a defiant hope rather than nihilism. The phrase ʾaḵ-ḏĕrāḵay ʾel-pānāyw ʾôḵîaḥ — 'but I will defend my ways before his face' — is unambiguous: Job intends to argue his case regardless of the outcome, which presupposes hope rather than its absence.
16: gam-hûʾ lî lîšûʿāh — 'this itself will become my salvation' — gam-hûʾ ('even this' or 'he himself also') marks the paradox: the very confrontation with the God who might kill him (v15) will be the source of his vindication/salvation. kî-lō-lĕp̄ānāyw ḥānēp̄ yāḇôʾ — 'for the godless cannot come before his presence' — ḥānēp̄ is the impious/godless person (used 13x in Job); Job's argument is that his willingness to present himself before God is itself evidence he is not godless, since hypocrites cannot endure divine scrutiny.
17: šimĕʿû šāmôaʿ millātî — 'hear carefully my declaration' — šāmôaʿ is the infinitive absolute intensifying šimĕʿû: truly/attentively hear. ûʾaḥwāṯî bĕʾoznêḵem — 'and my declaration into your ears' — ʾaḥwāh is to declare/show (from the root ḥwh, to show); Job is making a formal declaration that requires a formal hearing. The verse functions as a legal preamble to his ordered legal case in v18.
18: hinnēh-nāʾ ʿāraḵtî mišpāṭ — 'look, I have ordered my case' — ʿāraḵ mišpāṭ is technical legal vocabulary: to marshal/arrange one's legal brief (ʿāraḵ = to set in order, as in battle array or the Tabernacle showbread; mišpāṭ = judgment/case). Job has prepared his legal case with the same deliberateness as setting up for a legal proceeding. yāḏaʿtî kî-ʾāniʾ ʾeṣdāq — 'I know that I will be justified/vindicated' — the verb ṣādaq in the nip'al means to be proved righteous or vindicated; Job's certainty of ultimate vindication is the basis of his courage.
19: mî-hûʾ yārîḇ ʿimmî — 'who is there who will bring a charge against me?' — another legal formula; rîḇ + ʿim = to bring a dispute/charge against. The question is both a challenge (who can oppose me?) and an expression of Job's longing: he wants an adversary to appear so the case can be tried and won. kî-ʿattāh ʾaḥărîš wĕʾegwaʿ — 'for then I would be silent and die' — the conditional of despair: if no one can bring a charge and clear the air, Job would rather die in silence. The verse frames his speech as the only alternative to death by unresolved suffering.
20: ʾaḵ šĕtayim ʾal-taʿaś ʿimmî — 'only do not do these two things to me' — Job makes a precise and limited request of God. ʾāz mippāneyḵā lō ʾessātēr — 'and then I will not hide myself from your presence' — the condition for Job's direct engagement with God is the removal of two impositions (named in v21): the divine hand and the divine dread. The hiddenness/presence language echoes 13:24 (why does God hide his face?) — Job's offer to stop hiding is contingent on God removing what makes his presence unbearable.
21: kappĕḵā mēʿālay harḥēq — 'your hand — remove it far from me' — the divine hand (kap, palm/hand) as the instrument of affliction; removing it is the first condition. wĕʾêmāṯĕḵā ʾal-tĕḇahălannî — 'and do not let your terror overwhelm me' — ʾêmāh is numinous dread/terror (cf. 9:34; 33:7); bāhal in the piel means to terrify or throw into panic. Job cannot mount a rational legal case if God's terrifying presence overwrites his ability to think and speak coherently.
22: ûqrāʾ wĕʾānōḵî ʾeʿĕneh — 'or let me speak first and you answer me' — the two alternatives for structuring the divine-human dialogue. Job is willing either to respond to God's call or to initiate and let God respond. The symmetry is striking: Job as respondent OR Job as initiator, both equally acceptable — the crucial thing is that the encounter happens. This is the most direct assertion of dialogic reciprocity between Job and God in the entire book.
23: kammāh lî ʿăwōnôṯ waḥaṭṭāʾôṯ — 'how many are my iniquities and sins?' — ʿāwōn (moral iniquity, guilt) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (missing the mark, sin) are the two most common OT sin vocabulary words; Job's question is not rhetorical denial but a genuine desire to know the specific charges against him. pišʿî wĕḥaṭṭāʾṯî hôḏîʿēnî — 'make my transgression and my sin known to me' — pešaʿ is rebellion/willful transgression (the most morally serious of the three terms); Job uses all three sin-words together, covering the full spectrum, as if to say: whatever I have done, just show me what it is.
24: lāmmāh-p̄āneyḵā tastîr — 'why do you hide your face?' — the divine face-hiding (hester pānîm) is a major OT theological motif (Ps 13:1; 27:9; Isa 8:17; 45:15); it denotes divine withdrawal that leaves the sufferer in darkness and abandonment. Job is calling God to account for a hidden-face experience that he has done nothing to merit. wĕtiḥšĕḇēnî lĕʾôyēḇ lāḵ — 'and regard me as your enemy?' — ʾôyēḇ (enemy); Job protests that God's behavior toward him is indistinguishable from how one treats a hostile enemy.
25: hăʿaleh nidāp̄ tĕʿārôṣ — 'will you pursue a wind-driven leaf?' — nādap̄ means blown/driven (as in Isa 19:7 of reeds by a river); the image is of a completely powerless, insubstantial object that poses no threat. wĕʾet-qǎš yābēš tirḏōp̄ — 'and chase after dry stubble?' — qāš is the chaff/stubble; rādap̄ is to pursue with hostile intent (the verb for persecution). The incongruity of the Almighty chasing a dried leaf and stubble makes Job's suffering appear cosmically disproportionate to any supposed offense.
26: kî-ṯiḵtōḇ ʿālay merōrôṯ — 'for you write bitter things against me' — the image of written charges is a legal motif; God is cast as a plaintiff filing a formal written indictment (mĕrōrôṯ = bitter things/bitter charges). wĕṯôrîšēnî ʿăwōnôṯ nĕʿûrāy — 'and saddle me with the sins of my youth' — the imputed sins of youth (nōʿar = youth) are the charge-content; Job protests that God is charging him with offenses from an earlier life, a form of injustice since the normal moral logic assumes such debts have been settled or left behind.
27: wĕtāśem bassad raglāy — 'you put my feet in stocks' — sāḏ is the stock or wooden restraint for prisoners; applied to a righteous man it is a judicial travesty. ûtiśmōr kol-ʾorḥōṯāy — 'and keep watch over all my paths' — šāmar in the sense of surveillance rather than protection (the same verb used for guarding/keeping in Gen 3:24); God as warden rather than shepherd. ʿal-šorĕšê raglay tĕṯḥaqqeh — 'you mark the prints of my feet' — ḥāqaq means to inscribe/engrave; God etching the footprint-marks as permanent evidence, as one documents the movements of a prisoner.
28: wĕhûʾ kĕrāqāḇ yibleh — 'and he wastes away like a rotten thing' — rāqāḇ is decay/rottenness (wood-rot or general decomposition); the shift to third-person 'he' from the preceding 'I' signals that v28 either summarizes Job's condition from outside or is a scribal connection. kĕbegeḏ ʾăḵālô ʿāš — 'like a garment eaten by moths' — the moth-eating-garment metaphor for gradual, invisible deterioration; the same image appears in Job 4:19; Isa 50:9; 51:8; and Matt 6:19. The chapter closes on an image of quiet dissolution rather than dramatic collapse.
